catalog_details.title: Wingspread: The Pioneering of Aviation in New Zealand
catalog_details.author: Leo White

provenance_data.geospatial_relevance: Property constructed c. 1900; matches the geographic flight path of the 1942 Japanese overflight mentioned in the ephemera.

associated_ephemera.subject: Port of Auckland and Onehunga Coastal Shipping Timetable
key_entities_mentioned: Union Steam Ship Company (Colliers: Omana, Karepo)
Northern Steamship Company (Vessels: Hauturu, Ranginui, Clansman)
associated_ephemera.historical_context: Pre-lockdown shipping notice. Detailed commercial timetables like this were banned shortly after by the Directorate of Publicity under wartime censorship to hide ship movements from enemy submarines.

associated_ephemera.subject: Japanese Reconnaissance Overflight Eyewitness Report
associated_ephemera.eyewitness: Mr. E. Berg (Dominion Road Extension, Mt Eden area)
associated_ephemera.historical_incident: Imperial Japanese Navy pilot Nobuo Fujita launched a Yokosuka E14Y 'Glen' floatplane from submarine I-25 in the Hauraki Gulf, flying over Auckland undetected by military defenses.
flight_path_landmarks_in_text: Home of the Good Shepherd (Waikowhai Road, Mt Roskill)
associated_ephemera.propaganda_element: The clipping states the enemy plane was 'detected and pursued' by an aircraft from Whenuapai. Historical records show Auckland air defenses were caught completely off guard; the pursuit narrative was fabricated by censors to prevent public panic.
